title: Cedar Raised Garden Bed
summary: 4x8 cedar bed, no fasteners touching the soil that shouldn't be. Two hours, lasts a decade.
description:
The fastest useful thing in this catalog. Four boards, four corner posts, a box. What matters is the material choice, not the joinery.

Use cedar or redwood — untreated. **Do not use pressure-treated lumber for a bed you'll grow food in.** Modern ACQ treatment is far safer than the old arsenic-based CCA, but 'safer' is not 'inert', and cedar's natural rot resistance gets you 8–10 years anyway. Not worth the argument.

Also: no landscape fabric on the bottom. Leave it open to the soil so worms and roots can move through.

tools.note: A circular saw or even a hand saw is fine — these are simple square cuts.
Any saw that crosscuts a 2x8. Most stores will cut the boards to length for you.
Makes driving 3" screws into cedar much easier on your wrist.

materials.name: Cedar, 2x8, 8 ft
materials.note: ~$14 each. Two get cut in half for the ends. Untreated — see the description.

materials.name: Exterior structural screws, 3", coated
materials.note: Must be exterior-rated. Plain steel screws will rust through and stain the cedar within a season.

materials.name: Hardware cloth, 1/2" mesh (optional)
materials.note: Stapled to the bottom only if you have gophers or voles. Otherwise leave the bottom open.

cutList.note: Sit inside the corners. Leave 3" proud at the bottom to stake into the ground if you like.

steps.title: Cut everything
steps.body:
Two 8-ft boards stay full length. Cut two more to 45". Cut the 4x4 into four 11" posts.

That's the entire cut list. If the store will crosscut for you, take them up on it.

steps.title: Level the site first
steps.body:
Do this before assembly, not after. Clear the grass, and get the ground reasonably flat.

A raised bed on sloped ground fills unevenly and its corners pull apart over a couple of seasons as the soil pushes on the low side.

steps.title: Assemble the corners
steps.body:
Stand a long side and an end piece together in an L. Set a corner post inside the corner, flush with the top edge.

Drive four 3" exterior screws through each board into the post — two per board face. That's 8 screws per corner, and it is plenty.

steps.title: Close the box
steps.body:
Repeat for all four corners. Check the diagonals — equal diagonals means square, and square means the lid or any future trim will actually fit.

A few degrees out of square is fine here. Soil doesn't care.

steps.title: Set it and fill it
steps.body:
Place the bed. If you have burrowing pests, staple hardware cloth across the bottom now.

Fill with a mix roughly 60% topsoil, 30% compost, 10% aeration (perlite or coarse bark). A 4x8x7" bed takes about 16 cubic feet — order a yard if you're building more than one, it's far cheaper than bags.

Don't seal or finish the cedar. It will silver to grey and that's exactly what you want.
